sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A research team created an 8-item stress scale for prehealth students. Cronbach’s alpha was 0.84. A two-week test-retest correlation was 0.79. Scores correlated 0.68 with an established stress inventory and -0.42 with average nightly sleep. Students taking the scale during finals scored higher than students taking it during the first week of class. The team argued that the scale had evidence for reliability and convergent validity.
questions.stem: According to the figure and passage, which pattern was observed?

choices.B: Cronbach’s alpha and test-retest correlation provided reliability evidence
choices.C: The groups or conditions were identical
choices.D: The passage did not report any outcome differences

explanations.B: Correct. The passage identifies this specific relationship: Cronbach’s alpha and test-retest correlation provided reliability evidence. This is the central empirical pattern around which the study or scenario is organized, and it is the pattern the later interpretation depends on.
explanations.A: The reported evidence does not support the reverse pattern.
explanations.C: The passage describes meaningful differences rather than identical outcomes.
explanations.D: The passage gives concrete outcomes or interview findings, so differences were reported.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Reliability and validity coefficients for the new stress scale.

questions.stem: Which concept best explains the scenario?

explanations.A: Correct. The scenario is best explained by reliability and validity because the key issue is that Internal consistency and stability over two weeks address whether the instrument measures consistently. This connects the data to a core P/S framework rather than treating the finding as an isolated fact.
explanations.B: Classical conditioning is too narrow or unrelated to the social or cognitive process emphasized here.
explanations.C: Sensory adaptation concerns reduced responsiveness to constant sensory input, not the mechanism described.
explanations.D: The bystander effect concerns failures to help in groups and is not the main process in this passage.

questions.stem: What mechanism most plausibly accounts for the pattern?

choices.C: Internal consistency and stability over two weeks address whether the instrument measures consistently.
choices.A: A random change in DNA sequence during the study
choices.B: A complete absence of social context
choices.D: The physical impossibility of changing behavior

explanations.C: Correct. The passage points to this mechanism directly: Internal consistency and stability over two weeks address whether the instrument measures consistently. It explains why the observed groups or conditions differ and gives students a way to connect the empirical details to MCAT-level behavioral science.
explanations.A: The passage does not describe mutation or biological inheritance as the explanation.
explanations.B: The scenario is explicitly embedded in social, clinical, or developmental context.
explanations.D: The passage shows behavior or experience varying across conditions, so change is possible.

questions.stem: Which application would best follow from the passage?

choices.D: Use the scale with awareness of context because finals-week scores may differ from baseline-semester scores.
choices.A: Ignore the context and focus only on individual blame
choices.B: Use the least specific intervention possible
choices.C: Assume the finding applies identically to every person without adaptation

explanations.D: Correct. The best application is to Use the scale with awareness of context because finals-week scores may differ from baseline-semester scores. This response targets the mechanism described in the passage rather than relying on blame, vague advice, or one-size-fits-all reasoning.
explanations.A: Individual blame misses the contextual mechanism described in the passage.
explanations.B: A nonspecific intervention would not address the particular barrier or process identified.
explanations.C: P/S findings often guide interventions, but they still need adaptation to context and individual differences.

questions.stem: What is the most important limitation to keep in mind?

choices.C: The correlations support validity evidence but do not prove the scale captures every dimension of stress.
choices.A: The passage cannot be interpreted because it contains words rather than equations
choices.B: Any behavioral study is automatically causal
choices.D: The topic is outside the Psychological/Social Foundations section

explanations.C: Correct. The key limitation is that The correlations support validity evidence but do not prove the scale captures every dimension of stress. This keeps the inference aligned with the design and prevents overclaiming beyond what the passage can support.
explanations.A: P/S passages often use verbal descriptions and interviews; that does not make them uninterpretable.
explanations.B: Behavioral studies vary in design, and causal inference depends on assignment, controls, and measurement.
explanations.D: The concepts tested here are directly relevant to the P/S section.

questions.passage: A randomized study tested whether daily mood check-ins improved help-seeking. Students were assigned to a mood-check app or a neutral campus-news app for 21 days. Counseling-resource clicks averaged 2.8 in the mood-check group and 1.1 in control. Mean self-reflection ratings were 7.2 versus 5.4. The researchers tracked app use and found that students who opened the mood-check app more often clicked resources more often.
questions.stem: According to the figure and passage, which pattern was observed?

choices.B: The mood-check group clicked more counseling resources than the control group
choices.C: The groups or conditions were identical
choices.D: The passage did not report any outcome differences

explanations.B: Correct. The passage identifies this specific relationship: The mood-check group clicked more counseling resources than the control group. This is the central empirical pattern around which the study or scenario is organized, and it is the pattern the later interpretation depends on.
explanations.A: The reported evidence does not support the reverse pattern.
explanations.C: The passage describes meaningful differences rather than identical outcomes.
explanations.D: The passage gives concrete outcomes or interview findings, so differences were reported.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Counseling-resource clicks and self-reflection ratings by app assignment.

questions.stem: Which concept best explains the scenario?

explanations.A: Correct. The scenario is best explained by random assignment and adherence because the key issue is that Random assignment strengthens causal inference, while app-use logs show whether the intervention was actually used. This connects the data to a core P/S framework rather than treating the finding as an isolated fact.
explanations.B: Classical conditioning is too narrow or unrelated to the social or cognitive process emphasized here.
explanations.C: Sensory adaptation concerns reduced responsiveness to constant sensory input, not the mechanism described.
explanations.D: The bystander effect concerns failures to help in groups and is not the main process in this passage.

questions.stem: What mechanism most plausibly accounts for the pattern?

choices.D: Random assignment strengthens causal inference, while app-use logs show whether the intervention was actually used.
choices.A: A random change in DNA sequence during the study
choices.B: A complete absence of social context
choices.C: The physical impossibility of changing behavior

explanations.D: Correct. The passage points to this mechanism directly: Random assignment strengthens causal inference, while app-use logs show whether the intervention was actually used. It explains why the observed groups or conditions differ and gives students a way to connect the empirical details to MCAT-level behavioral science.
explanations.A: The passage does not describe mutation or biological inheritance as the explanation.
explanations.B: The scenario is explicitly embedded in social, clinical, or developmental context.
explanations.C: The passage shows behavior or experience varying across conditions, so change is possible.

questions.stem: Which application would best follow from the passage?

choices.B: Compare outcomes by assigned condition and also examine adherence without replacing the randomized analysis.
choices.A: Ignore the context and focus only on individual blame
choices.C: Use the least specific intervention possible
choices.D: Assume the finding applies identically to every person without adaptation

explanations.B: Correct. The best application is to Compare outcomes by assigned condition and also examine adherence without replacing the randomized analysis. This response targets the mechanism described in the passage rather than relying on blame, vague advice, or one-size-fits-all reasoning.
explanations.A: Individual blame misses the contextual mechanism described in the passage.
explanations.C: A nonspecific intervention would not address the particular barrier or process identified.
explanations.D: P/S findings often guide interventions, but they still need adaptation to context and individual differences.

questions.stem: What is the most important limitation to keep in mind?

choices.A: Adherence differences can complicate interpretation because high users may differ from low users in motivation.
choices.B: The passage cannot be interpreted because it contains words rather than equations
choices.C: Any behavioral study is automatically causal
choices.D: The topic is outside the Psychological/Social Foundations section

explanations.A: Correct. The key limitation is that Adherence differences can complicate interpretation because high users may differ from low users in motivation. This keeps the inference aligned with the design and prevents overclaiming beyond what the passage can support.
explanations.B: P/S passages often use verbal descriptions and interviews; that does not make them uninterpretable.
explanations.C: Behavioral studies vary in design, and causal inference depends on assignment, controls, and measurement.
explanations.D: The concepts tested here are directly relevant to the P/S section.
